coredata.dc:description: Solar-driven interfacial evaporation desalination has emerged as a promising strategy for alleviating the water scarcity crisis. However, the design of evaporators with excellent heat management and minimal heat loss to achieve high-efficiency clean water production remains a crucial challenge. Herein, inspired by the transpiration of mushrooms in nature, we demonstrated a bionic-structured hydrogel evaporator, fabricated with biomass lotus root starch (LR), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), and ink-modified carbon quantum dots and TiO2 (i-CQDs-TiO2). The evaporated water can be instantly replenished during evaporation through the internal water circulation of the “stipe”, and the special structural design reduces the transfer of heat to the water. Therefore, the evaporator demonstrates excellent performance, with a pure water evaporation rate of up to 3.78 kg·m−2·h−1 and an efficiency of 98% (1 kW·m−2). More importantly, the evaporator has excellent salt resistance with an average evaporation rate of 3.49 kg·m−2·h−1 in 1–25 wt% brine. In addition, without salt scaling on the evaporation surface in 10 wt% brine for continuous 5 h of illumination. Furthermore, due to the coordinated photocatalysis of i-CQDs and TiO2, the catalytic degradation rates of TB and CR were up to 91.8% and 88.9%, respectively. With an efficient and stable desalination capacity, this solar evaporator with a bionic design offers a new pathway for sustainable solar desalination.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar-driven interfacial evaporation desalination has emerged as a promising strategy for alleviating the water scarcity crisis. However, the design of evaporators with excellent heat management and minimal heat loss to achieve high-efficiency clean water production remains a crucial challenge. Herein, inspired by the transpiration of mushrooms in nature, we demonstrated a bionic-structured hydrogel evaporator, fabricated with biomass lotus root starch (LR), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), and ink-modified carbon quantum dots and TiO2 (i-CQDs-TiO2). The evaporated water can be instantly replenished during evaporation through the internal water circulation of the “stipe”, and the special structural design reduces the transfer of heat to the water. Therefore, the evaporator demonstrates excellent performance, with a pure water evaporation rate of up to 3.78 kg·m−2·h−1 and an efficiency of 98% (1 kW·m−2). More importantly, the evaporator has excellent salt resistance with an average evaporation rate of 3.49 kg·m−2·h−1 in 1–25 wt% brine. In addition, without salt scaling on the evaporation surface in 10 wt% brine for continuous 5 h of illumination. Furthermore, due to the coordinated photocatalysis of i-CQDs and TiO2, the catalytic degradation rates of TB and CR were up to 91.8% and 88.9%, respectively. With an efficient and stable desalination capacity, this solar evaporator with a bionic design offers a new pathway for sustainable solar desalination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water scarcity is now a critical issue that has become a major concern for the public [1–3]. According to the United Nations, more than two billion people are currently facing a water shortage crisis and this number is going to grow in the future years [4]. In this context, the solar-driven interfacial evaporation (SIE) has been proposed as a promising technology, which uses solar energy to evaporate and convert various brine (seawater, brackish water [5], wastewater [6], etc.) into clean water for human consumption [7–16]. In recent years, hydrogels have been widely used as photothermal conversion materials due to their unique three-dimensional skeletal structure, special hydrophilic properties, and excellent chemical and physical properties [2,17–21]. However, the evaporation performance of most hydrogel evaporators still does not meet the requirements of practical desalination applications. The main reasons are the heat loss of the materials and the problem of surface salt deposition [22].

The heat losses involved in the evaporation process are mainly thermal radiation losses at the hydrogel-air interface and top-down thermal conduction losses [23,24]. And excessive heat loss to the bulk water can break the equilibrium between water transport and evaporation on the hydrogel surface, ultimately resulting in the formation of a thick liquid film that exacerbates the adhesion of contaminants and salt crystals [25]. Furthermore, the accumulation of salt particles on the surface will reflect incident light and hinder water evaporation, significantly compromising the evaporation performance [8,26–28]. Recently, directional salt precipitator evaporators have been proposed, which enable the salt scale to appear only at the edge of the evaporator by changing the direction of salt ion transport [28–30]. Nevertheless, even though such evaporators reduce the effect of salt scale on evaporation, it also results in insufficient vertical transport channels for the brine, thereby reducing the evaporation rate. Besides, Janus hydrogels [31–34], polyzwitterionic hydrogels [35,36], and biomass hydrogels [37–41] have been designed to address salt deposition on the surface of materials [10]. Although these types of hydrogels can reduce surface salt to a certain extent, they cannot effectively reduce the vertical heat conduction generated during the long and continuous desalination process, especially in high salt concentrations. Therefore, how establishing a topological structure of hydrogel that can simultaneously localize heat and expel salt ions is key to achieving stable evaporation performance in the treatment of high salinity wastewater [42,43].

Mushrooms are plants in nature characterized by a large cross-sectional black pileus and a fibrous stipe. The stipe can instantly provide an adequate water supply to the pileus through internal water circulation. Meanwhile, its porous structure provides a water transport pathway. In addition, the special structure can establish a heat transfer boundary for the water transport layer, thus minimizing heat transfer losses (Fig. 1 ). Drawing inspiration from this natural phenomenon, a bionic mushroom-structured hydrogel evaporator based on lotus root starch (LR) [44]/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [45] was constructed (PLBH). The stipe serves as a reliable source of water supply and facilitates the upward transfer of water through capillary action. To improve the performance of the system, a larger cross-sectional evaporative layer was utilized to reduce heat loss to the bottom water, and i-CQDs-TiO2 is incorporated into the top pileus to enhance solar energy absorption and achieve photocatalytic efficacy. Based on the excellent synergistic performance of brine transport and photothermal management, PLBH demonstrates exceptional salt resistance. In other word, PLBH can redissolve salt during desalination, and preventing salt particles from accumulating on the surface. PLBH exhibited an evaporation rate of up to 3.78 kg·m−2·h−1 under 1 solar light intensity (1 kW·m−2) and evaporation remained stable and efficient over a wide range of pH and salinity ranges. Specifically, the average evaporation rate was 3.49 kg·m−2·h−1 in 1–25 wt% brine, and no salt scaling on the evaporation surface was observed for 5 h of continuous solar exposure. Furthermore, the degradation of TB and CR by PLBH was more than 85%, which is attributed to the coordinated action of TiO2 and i-CQDs. These results highlight the potential of PLBH as a promising solution for the desalination of brine and will further advance the practical application of solar desalination.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), hydrochloric acid (HCl), H2SO4, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Titanium oxide (TiO2, 99.8%), and glutaraldehyde (GA, 50%) were purchased from Aladdin. Trypan Blue (TB), Congo red (CR), Sodium chloride (NaCl), Glycerol (GL), and polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG) were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. Lotus root starch (LR) was obtained from Shandong Aicai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Chinese ink (CI) was purchased from Chunxi Cultural Goods Factory. The real seawater samples come from the South China Sea.

CQDs nanofluids were modified using CI to enhance solar light absorption. Specifically, PEG-200, GL, and CI were mixed in the ratio of 3:1:0.2 by volume and stirred magnetically for 30 min to obtain the precursor solution. Then the precursor solution was heated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 30 min to obtain the i-CQDs nanofluids. The 0.01 g of TiO2 was dispersed in 5 mL of i-CQDs nanofluids and stirred for 4 h to obtain i-CQD-TiO2 mixed solution.

The hydrogels were prepared by chemical cross-linking, and the preparation of PLBH is displayed in Fig. 2 . The 2 g of PVA and 1 g of LR were added to 15 mL of deionized water at 85 °C (solution A). Then, 5 mL of i-CQDs-TiO2 mixed solution was added to solution A and labeled it as solution B. GA (0.125 mL, 5%), and HCl solution (0.25 mL, 1.2 mol/L) were added to 10 mL of solution B and labeled it as solution C. Subsequently, transferred solution C to the mold and the evaporated layer of PLBH was obtained. The water transfer layer of PLBH was prepared in the same way, but without the addition of i-CQD-TiO2. Finally, the evaporation and water transport layers were combined before gelation, the complete PLBH was obtained.

The skeleton structure of hydrogels was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JOEL JSM7610F) equipped with EDS. The chemical composition was characterized by FT-IR (Thermo Fisher Nicolet Is10). The XRD (D8 advance, Germany) was used to describe the crystal structure. The UV–vis-NIR spectrometer (UV3600 plus) was used to characterize the absorbance of hydrogel. The freeze-drying device was used Shanghai LICHEN-BX Instrument Technology CO., Ltd (LC-10 N).

The evaporation experiments were carried out at a temperature of 25±1 °C and a humidity of 55%. First, the different solar radiation intensities (0.3, 0.5, 0.7, and 1 sun) were simulated by a xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300). A beaker (4 cm in diameter) filled with 40 mL of deionized water was put on an electronic balance. The hydrogel was floated in the beaker and placed under the simulated solar source (The device as displayed in Fig S10). A solar radiometer measured and adjusted the intensity of solar received on the hydrogel surface. Mass loss was recorded every 10 min for 2 h, with the first hour being the stabilization time. Then, an infrared thermal imager is then used to photograph temperature changes on the surface of the hydrogel.
See section S1 for details.

Saltwater samples with different concentrations (1, 5, 10, 25 wt%) and saline (adjusted in the saltwater of 10 wt%) wastewater with different pH values (pH = 3, 5, 9, and 11) were prepared to test the desalination capacity of PLBH on saline wastewater. The experimental procedure simply involves replacing the deionized water in section 2.5 with the above wastewater. The difference was that evaporated water before and after desalination was collected for water quality testing. Specifically, the total dissolved solids (TDS) of saltwater samples were determined. In addition, the concentration of salt ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+) from the South China Sea was traced by ICP-OES, and the change in pHs of the wastewater after purification were measured.

The two typical dyes, 80 mL of Congo Red (CR, 50 ppm) and Trypan Blue (TB, 25 ppm) were prepared to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of PLBH. First, 1 h dark adsorption experiment was carried out to reach the adsorption equilibrium of the hydrogel. Then, the photocatalytic experiments were started by turning on the light source (xenon lamp, 1 kW·m−2) and collecting samples every 1 h. The photocatalytic experiments were carried out for 4 h and the absorbance was finally measured by UV–Vis.

In the photocatalytic degradation of dyes, Reactive species trapping experiments were carried out in which Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ), and disodium EDTA (EDTA-2Na) were used as trapping agents for hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide radicals (O2 −) and photogenerated holes (h+) respectively.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the PLBH comprises two layers on top and bottom, including a black solar-absorbing layer (“mushroom pileus”) and a water transport layer (“mushroom stipe”). During the process of evaporation, the evaporation layer absorbs sunlight and converts it into thermal energy, thereby evaporating the water molecules beneath it. Meanwhile, the water can be efficiently replenished through the interconnected and porous network within the transport layer, thereby ensuring a continuous and efficient supply of water for the process of evaporation. In comparison with traditional Janus hydrogel evaporators, the PLBH evaporator reduced the surface area of the water delivery layer, thereby effectively mitigating heat dissipation downwards or into the adjacent bottom water. This enables the realization of localized surface heating and enhancements in evaporation performance. The morphological and microstructural characteristics of PLBH were investigated using SEM. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the water transport layer exhibits interconnected channels ranging from a few to several tens of microns, with neatly arranged pores. At higher magnification (Fig. 3(e)), the inner surface of the pores can be seen to be smooth. In addition, the “hydrophilic tentacles” (red circles) are prominently visible along the marginal areas of the pore channels. These pores and “water tentacles” facilitate the uptake and transport of water and provide sufficient water for the evaporation process. The evaporated layer maintained the pore-like skeleton structure (Fig. 3(b)). Meanwhile, the attachment of i-CQDs-TiO2 to the pore walls (blue circle) enhances the surface roughness, which will enhance the solar absorption performance. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of a map scan of the selected region in Fig S1(a). C, O, and Ti elements can be observed throughout the region (Fig S1(b)), indicating that i-CQDs-TiO2 is successfully encapsulated in the polymer backbone. Fig. 3(f) illustrates the FT-IR analysis conducted on the hydrogel. The spectrum of PLBH exhibits broad absorption peaks at the range of 3400–3600 cm−1 is O-H stretching vibration, and the peak observed at 2934 cm−1 is C-H stretching vibrations [1]. The peaks at 2880 cm−1 is C=C stretching vibration peaks of i-CQDs [46]. Additionally, the peaks observed at 1730 cm−1 is the C=O stretching vibrations in PVA and i-CQDs [47], whereas the peaks appearing at 1642 cm−1 is attributed to C-O stretching vibration peaks in LR and i-CQDs [48]. The feature peak at 1262 cm−1 is associated with C-O-C in i-CQDs. The FT-IR spectra analysis unequivocally confirms the successful preparation of PLBH. The XRD spectra of TiO2 and PLBH are shown in Fig. 3(g). The 2θ values of TiO2 are observed at 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.1°, 53.9°, 55.1°, and 62.7°. These characteristic peaks are also present in PLBH, indicating that TiO2 was successfully doped into the hydrogel. Furthermore, Hydrogel is a viscoelastic material, the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G") can reflect their mechanical properties [49] and excellent mechanical properties are a prerequisite for its stable operation in seawater [50]. Therefore, the rheological property test was tested at 25 °C. The results show that the hydrogels have a wide linear viscoelastic region, which reveals their crosslinked state. In addition, G' is larger than G", which confirms the crosslinked backbone of the polymer, indicating many internal crosslinking points and excellent mechanical properties (Fig S2).

The evaporation performance of PVA/LR (PLH), Janus (PLJH), and PLBH hydrogel evaporators was evaluated and compared (Fig S3). The mass loss of water during evaporation was recorded under one solar intensity exposure and a typical curve was plotted in Fig. 4 (a), with the slope of the curve representing the evaporation rate. In Fig. 4(b), the evaporation rate of PLJH (2.82 kg·m−2·h−1) is higher than PLH (2.33 kg·m−2·h−1) and pure water (0.45 kg·m−2·h−1). The results reveal that the utilization of a graded structure design can reduce heat loss and improve evaporation performance. Unfortunately, the conventional Janus-structured hydrogel evaporators were unable to avoid downward heat loss, so the bionic mushroom structured evaporator was constructed. First, the influence of the area and length of the mushroom rootstock on the evaporation rate was analyzed (Fig S5 and Table S1), and the optimal structure (A4) was determined. Because of the excellent water transfer capabilities of the PLBH “stipe”, sufficient water supply is ensured for the evaporation process. In addition, the special structural design of PLBH establishes a heat transfer boundary for the water transport layer, thereby minimizing heat transfer losses and facilitating the retention of most heat in the evaporation layer (Fig. 4(c) depicts the schematic diagram of the evaporation mechanism). Consequently, the evaporation rate of PLBH increased by 62.23% over compared with that of PLJH, reaching 3.78 kg·m−2·h−1. Meanwhile, an interesting evaporation experiment was carried out, in which the light source was deactivated at the end of the evaporation and the mass loss continued to be recorded for 1 h. As illustrated in Fig. 4(c), the evaporation rate for 1 h still reached 1.31 kg·m−2·h−1 after the removal of light, because partial heat retained in the evaporation layer for the residual heat utilization. Furthermore, the excellent surface heat localization capability and the advantage of low heat loss make PLBH achieve ideal rates even at 0.3, 0.5, and 0.7 solar intensities (Fig S6). The evaporation rates of PLBH at 0.7, 0.5, and 0.3 solar intensities was 2.742, 2.065, and 1.162 kg·m−2·h−1 respectively, which means that PLBH is suitable for almost all regions with different natural sunlight conditions. The evaporation efficiency (Fig S7) under 1 kW·m−2 is defined as Eq. S(1) [1,51] and S(2) [52].

The calculated evaporation efficiency of the PLBH evaporator is as high as 98.07%, which is much higher than that of the PLH (84.39%) and PLJH (90.25%). The above results demonstrate that the PLBH evaporator with a special bionic mushroom structure achieves excellent thermal localization and minimal heat loss (the energy loss analyzed in section S9 of the supplemental information), thereby efficiently producing solar steam, which makes it stand out among many bionic evaporators (Fig. 4(e)).

Furthermore, the total raw material cost of PLBH is only about $10.531 m−2 (supplemental information section S10). The cost-effectiveness ( ε ) here is defined by Eq. (1) [1] as:
(1)
ε = γ / c
where γ is the evaporation rate (kg·m−2·h−1) of PLBH, and c is the cost of raw materials ($/m2). Table S2 shows the cost-effectiveness values of the PLBH compared to other hydrogel evaporators. The results show that the PLBH has a cost-effectiveness value of 358.94 g/(h*$), which makes it stand out among many evaporators.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The water transfer and thermal management capabilities
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface contact angles of the freeze-dried PLBH were measured. In Fig. 5 (a), the contact angles of the water transport and evaporation layers is 72.87° and 75.73° respectively. The results indicate that the incorporation of i-CQDs-TiO2 has minimal impact on the hydrophilic properties of the hydrogel and that both layers possess suitable hydrophilicity. In fact, overly high hydrophilicity will lead to the formation of a heavy water film on the evaporating surface, which will restrict heat exchange. However, if the surface is too hydrophobic, it can’t be wetted easily, which will result in the reduction of evaporation performance [56]. Therefore, an attainment appropriate hydrophilicity can facilitate the wetting of the PLBH surface while preventing formation of the excessively thick water film. The saturated water content (
Q S
) of PLBH was tested by Eq. (2) [9,57].
(2)
Q S
= W /
W d
where
W d
is the initial weight of the freeze-dried hydrogel and W is the weight after water absorption and swelling. The
Q S
for the top and bottom layers in PLBH is both around 6 g/g, which is consistent with the contact angle measurements.

The molecular dynamics (MD) simulations [58] were conducted to evaluate the evaporation process of water on the PLBH surface. The evaporation model construction is shown in Fig S8. PLBH, with suitable hydrophilicity, can allow all water molecules to pass through the evaporating surface, which is consistent with experimental observations. The simulation results are shown in Fig. 5(b). 4.36% of the water molecules escaped through the bottom of the hydrogel within 10 ns, escaped from the PLBH surface and evaporated into the air. Thus, the effect of PLBH on the evaporation behavior of water molecules at the microscopic level was revealed by MD simulations. The PVA/LR chains can control the distribution of water at the evaporation interface, while the hydrophilic surface can increase the water evaporation rate of the hydrogel.

The solar absorption characteristic of the PLBH evaporation layer was evaluated by UV–Vis-NIR. As depicted in Fig. 5(c), with the incorporation of i-CQDs-TiO2 in the evaporation layer, PLBH exhibits exceptional solar absorption (up to 95%) in the 200 to 2500 nm. This outstanding solar absorption capacity enables PLBH to maximize solar energy absorption, thereby enhancing heat exchange and promoting evaporation. The ideal evaporation performance of the PLBH is related to its excellent thermal management and thermal localization capabilities. Therefore, the thermal conductivities of the pileus and stipe were assessed. The results show that the thermal conductivities are 0.52 and 0.32 W⋅m−1⋅K−1, respectively (Fig. 5(d)). The water transfer layer, with lower thermal conductivity, serves as a “barrier” to heat transfer, thereby minimizing downward heat loss. To investigate the thermal management capacity of PLBH under 1 solar intensity, a multi-threaded thermocouple and an infrared thermal imager were employed to track the temperature of the evaporating surface and bulk water. As displayed in Fig. 5(e)–(g), the temperature response of PLBL is higher than that of PLH and PLJH, with the surface temperature of the water rapidly rising to approximately 38.3 °C within 10 min, and the maximum equilibrium temperature exceeding 46 °C, while the temperature of the bulk water is only about 32 °C. Furthermore, the temperature difference between the surface and the bulk water of PLBH measures approximately 15 °C, which far exceeds that of PLH and PLJH (Fig S9(a) and (b)). This result indicates that the mushroom-structured evaporator, with exceptional heat management capabilities, can promote heat localization. For this reason, with more heat being used to evaporate water, the evaporator can enhance evaporation performance, which is consistent with the evaporation rate measurement results. The temperature distributions of PLBH (Fig. 5(h) and Fig S9(c)) and PLJH (Fig. 5(i) and Fig S9(d)) at steady state were simulated with Fluent to further evaluate the thermal management capability of the evaporator. The results show that the PLBH exhibits a greater ability to locate heat, while the PLJH transfers most of its heat below. It indicates that the mushroom structure evaporator confines most of the heat to the evaporating surface, thereby minimizing heat loss and promoting the steam generation, which is consistent with the experimental results.

The structural design effectively prevents the formation of an excessively thick liquid film on the surface, thereby mitigating the surface accumulation of salt and the adhesion of contaminants. To corroborate these suppositions, the influence of salt concentration on the evaporation performance of the biomimetic mushroom evaporator was evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 6 (a), the evaporation rate of the PLBH in brines of differing salinities was ascertained by monitoring the mass loss of the brine. The evaporation rate of PLBH exhibits a marginal decline as the salt concentration increases. Specifically, the evaporation rates observed for the various brine, containing 1, 5, 10, and 25 wt% salts, are 3.63, 3.54, 3.46, and 3.31 kg·m−2·h−1, respectively. The average evaporation rate for the salt concentration tested was 3.49 kg·m−2·h−1, a slight decline of 7.8% in the evaporation rate compared to pure water (3.78 kg·m−2·h−1), which indicated the excellent salt resistance ability of PLBH. The stable and durable performance of an evaporator is essential for its reliable operation. To assess the continuous evaporation capabilities of PLBH, tests were conducted using 10 wt% brine subjected to 1 solar intensity for a duration of 5 h. Remarkably, as observed in Fig. 6(c), the surface of the PLBH remained free of salt crystals and other contaminants. Moreover, the long-term stability of PLBH was evaluated, and the results indicated that the evaporation rate remained almost constant during 15 days of cycling in 10 wt% concentration brine (Fig. 6(d)). These results illustrate the exceptional stability and long-term durability of PLBH, which enable its sustained operation over extended periods.

To assess the water quality after desalination by PLBH, the total dissolved solids (TDS) values were measured for four brine samples before and after treatment. As displayed in Fig. 6(e), the TDS values of the four brine samples were significantly decreased from 6720 (1 wt%), 53,600 (5 wt%), 125,700 (10 wt%), and 329,000 (25 wt%) mg/L to 27, 32, 47 and 39 mg/L. The values decreased by at least three orders of magnitude, which and the desalinated water met the drinking water standards set forth by both the WHO (1000 mg/L) [59,60] and EPA (500 mg/L) [61,62]. Fig. 6(f) presents the concentrations of waste metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, and Ca2+) concentration in evaporated water from a real seawater sample. The ion content after desalination is lower than the standard set by the WHO, with a waste ion removal rate exceeding 99%. These results show that the desalination performance of PLBH is excellent and that the evaporated water fully meets the WHO drinking water standards. Apart from brine, the acid and base resistance of PLBH was evaluated in wastewater with pH values of 3, 5, 9, and 11, which were achieved by adjusting the NaOH and H2SO4 concentration in 10 wt% brine [63]. This range of pH values usually covers all domestic and industrial wastewater. As illustrated in Fig. 6(g)–(i), PLBH demonstrated stable evaporation performance across diverse pH conditions in salt wastewater, with only marginal reductions in evaporation rates compared to pure water and 10 wt% saltwater. Furthermore, the pH of the resulting evaporated water was in the neutral range for all samples (Fig. 6(i)), which satisfies the WHO drinking water standard of 6.5–8.5. The above results show that PLBH has excellent salt and acid-base resistance and promising potential for practical desalination applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic performance test of PLBH
ce:section.ce:para:
Most wastewater contains organic pollutants, and treatment using SIE alone may prove inadequate to meet practical requirements [64]. Consequently, the excellent photocatalytic characteristics of PLBH can be attributed to the synergistic interplay between i-CQDs and TiO2 [48]. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), when the energy absorbed by i-CQDs-TiO2 in the PLBH evaporation layer exceeds the band gap energy of the system, holes (h+) are generated in the conduction band (CB), and photogenerated electrons (e−) in the valence band (VB). i-CQDs possess excellent electron-conducting properties and can form chemical bonds with TiO2, thereby enabling the efficient transfer of photogenerated electrons and reducing the rate of recombination of photogenerated carriers [65]. The photogenerated electrons (e−) transferred to the i-CQDs combine with dissolved oxygen in the water to produce superoxide ion radicals (·O2 −), and the vacancies (h+) remaining in the VB of the TiO2 can react directly with the –OH radicals to form ·OH (Eqs. (3) and (4)). Subsequently, the O2 − and ·OH− participate in the degradation of the dyes, leading to the formation of CO2 and H2O as end products (Eq. (5)) [66,67].

During the photocatalytic process, photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) can be transferred to the interface to participate in redox reactions. Therefore, the contribution of different active substances to the photocatalytic degradation of TB was investigated. The Photocatalytic capture experiment was repeated using IPA, BQ and EDTA-2Na as trapping agents for ·OH, O2 − and h+, respectively. The results showed that the degradation rates of TB were all inhibited to a certain extent, indicating that all three active substances played a role, with O2 − playing the most significant role in the photocatalytic degradation process (Fig S11). Then, Trypan blue (TB) and Congo red (CR) were employed as model dye pollutants to comprehensively assess the photocatalytic performance of PLBH. Prior to photocatalysis, a one-hour dark adsorption experiment was conducted. As depicted in Fig. 7(c) and (d), the color of both dyes progressively lightened with increasing catalytic time, indicating the degradation of both dyes by PLBH (0′ is the dye after reaching adsorption equilibrium). Furthermore, the catalytic degradation of the dyes was quantitatively evaluated using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fig. 7(b) depicts the curve of the normalized concentration (C/C0) with time. The TB and CR were degraded by 48% and 37% during dark adsorption, indicating that PLBH possesses the ability to adsorb organic pollutants and reach the catalytic active sites, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance. The catalytic degradation rates of TB and CR were up to 91.8% and 88.9%, respectively, which is attributed to the coordinated photocatalysis of i-CQDs and TiO2 that effectively inhibited the recombination of photogenerated carriers. These results demonstrate the exceptional photocatalytic performance of PLBH.

The outdoor experiment was conducted under natural sunlight at Chengdu University of Technology from 8:00 to 17:00 on May 12, 2023. The device of the outdoor solar desalination experiment is shown in s Fig. 8 and Fig S12. The device contains water intake, water outlet, hydrogel placement plate, water collection tank and a roof-mounted spotlight top (the jointing process is shown in the support Video SI). And the hydrogel placement plate is made of plastic that can be stuck on the edge of the evaporator to prevent the hydrogel from sinking in the water. It can be replaced by foam material to reduce the cost in further applications. During the experiment, the evaporation rate steadily increased with increasing solar light intensity. Fig. 8 demonstrates that at 13:00p.m., the maximum radiation of 0.82 kW⋅m−2 was recorded alongside a peak evaporation rate of 2.69 kg·m−2·h−1. Furthermore, desalinated freshwater was collected simultaneously using the evaporator (Fig. 8(c)), with a daily water yield of 3.28 kg. The results indicate that PLBH can achieve high freshwater production.

In summary, a hydrogel evaporator with a bionic mushroom structure was developed. The unique structural design reduces water heat loss, enabling the evaporator to demonstrate excellent thermal positioning performance and efficient water transfer coordination. The mechanism of heat loss reduction of this structure is verified through experiments and numerical simulations. Furthermore, based on the above outstanding performance, the evaporator achieves an average evaporation rate of 3.49 kg⋅m−2⋅h−1 in 1–25 wt% brine under 1 sun illumination. Meanwhile, the evaporator can remove heavy metal ions and works stably in different acid and alkaline ranges. Moreover, PLBH can effectively degrade organic dyes with degradation rates of 91.8% and 88.9% for TB and CR, respectively. The designed bionic mushroom structure evaporator will further promote the practical application of solar desalination.
